packTitle: Weather and Environmental Exposure Import Batch
notes: Normalized from Perplexity category output for Weather and Environmental Exposure.
Heat, air-quality, smoke, UV, and cross-topic red-flag content is strong and should be ranked normally.
Cold, lightning, and altitude content is useful but some references are indirect or secondary; related guides are marked reviewed or needs_source_review accordingly.
This batch is designed to complement earlier Natural Disasters, Medical Safety, Shelter/Fire/Warmth, and Power/Utilities batches without deleting prior guides.

categories.summary: Practical guidance for heat, cold, smoke, dust, lightning, sun, altitude, and when weather exposure becomes a medical emergency.

guides.title: Everyday Extreme Heat Precautions (Non-Emergency)
guides.quickAnswer: Stay cool, hydrated, out of direct sun, and check on high-risk people during extreme heat.
guides.whenToUse: During heat advisories, heat waves, or any period when temperatures or heat index are much higher than normal and you are not yet acutely ill.
guides.preferredAction: Limit heat exposure, use cooler indoor spaces or shade, drink water regularly, and shift activity to cooler parts of the day.
guides.backupAction: If you have no air-conditioning, use fans when outdoor air is cooler, cross-ventilation, cool showers, damp cloths, and frequent shaded rest.
steps: Check local forecasts and heat alerts and plan outdoor tasks for early morning or evening.
Wear loose, light-colored clothing, a hat, and sunscreen on exposed skin.
Drink water in small, regular amounts rather than waiting until you feel very thirsty.
Limit alcohol, very sugary drinks, and heavy hot meals that can worsen dehydration or heat stress.
Use cool showers, baths, or damp cloths on neck, armpits, and groin if you feel overheated.
Check on older adults, infants, people with chronic disease, outdoor workers, and unhoused neighbors during heat waves.
Never leave children, disabled people, or pets in parked cars.
warnings: Heat can worsen heart, lung, kidney, and other chronic conditions before obvious heat-illness symptoms appear.
Dehydration can be harder to notice in older adults and children.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on thirst to decide when to drink.
Do not do strenuous activity during peak afternoon heat if it can be avoided.
Do not leave anyone in a closed parked vehicle.
redFlags: Confusion, fainting, chest pain, trouble breathing, or vomiting during heat exposure.
Symptoms suggesting severe dehydration or heat stroke.
preparednessTips: Identify the coolest room in your home and how to ventilate it safely.
Know locations of public cooling centers or air-conditioned public spaces.
Store extra drinking water and oral rehydration supplies if outages are common.

sourceReferences.title: About Heat and Your Health
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Overview of heat risks and general protective actions.

sourceReferences.title: Protect Yourself From the Dangers of Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical stay-cool and hydration guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Heat Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing hot weather behavior guidance.

guides.title: Recognizing and Treating Heat Exhaustion
guides.quickAnswer: Move the person to a cooler place, cool them down, and give small sips of fluid; seek care if symptoms worsen or last about an hour.
guides.whenToUse: When someone in the heat has heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, or dizziness but is still awake and able to drink.
guides.preferredAction: Cool the person promptly with shade or AC, wet cloths, and fluids, then monitor closely for worsening symptoms.
guides.backupAction: If you have no AC, use shade, loosen clothing, fan the person, and apply cool wet cloths while offering small sips of cool water if they can drink.
steps: Move the person to a cooler area, ideally indoors with air-conditioning or otherwise into shade.
Lay them down and loosen or remove excess clothing.
Apply cool wet cloths, mist the skin, or use a cool bath if available and safe.
Give cool water or oral rehydration solution in small frequent sips if the person is fully awake and not vomiting.
Stay with them and monitor for at least an hour; seek medical care if symptoms do not improve or worsen.
warnings: Heat exhaustion can progress to life-threatening heat stroke.
People with heart disease, kidney disease, or certain medications may need medical advice earlier.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol or very sugary drinks.
Do not leave the person alone or send them back to strenuous activity the same day.
redFlags: Confusion, loss of consciousness, seizures, chest pain, or trouble breathing.
Vomiting that prevents the person from keeping fluids down.
Very hot or dry skin suggesting heat stroke.
preparednessTips: Keep oral rehydration salts or electrolyte packets in first aid kits.
Teach early signs of heat illness to workers, athletes, and families.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to Heat Stroke (Medical Emergency)
guides.quickAnswer: Call emergency services immediately, move the person to a cooler place, and start rapid cooling; do not give fluids to a confused or unconscious person.
guides.whenToUse: When someone exposed to heat has very high body temperature, confusion, seizure, collapse, or altered consciousness.
guides.preferredAction: Activate emergency medical services at once and start aggressive cooling with cool water, wet sheets, and airflow.
guides.backupAction: If EMS is delayed, continue cooling with any safe method available and arrange urgent transport to medical care.
steps: Call emergency services as soon as heat stroke is suspected.
Move the person to the coolest accessible place and remove excess clothing.
Cool the body rapidly with cool water, wet cloths or sheets, or a cool bath if safe.
Fan the person while keeping the skin wet to increase evaporation.
If vomiting or unconscious, position on the side if safe and maintain the airway.
Continue active cooling until help arrives or the person is under medical supervision.
warnings: Heat stroke can cause permanent organ damage or death.
Rapid cooling and emergency care are critical.
whatNotToDo: Do not give fluids if the person is confused, vomiting, or unconscious.
Do not delay calling for help while trying home measures first.
Do not use alcohol rubs or extreme direct ice exposure over large areas as a substitute for standard cooling.
redFlags: Any confusion, slurred speech, seizure, loss of consciousness, or very hot body with heat exposure.
Collapse or inability to respond normally in the heat.
preparednessTips: Build a one-tap heat-stroke emergency action card in the app that emphasizes calling local emergency services first.
Use buddy checks for people working or exercising in high heat.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Describes heat stroke and rapid cooling.

guides.title: Working or Exercising Safely in High Heat
guides.quickAnswer: Increase water intake, schedule harder work for cooler times, take frequent shade breaks, and build up activity gradually in hot conditions.
guides.whenToUse: For outdoor workers, athletes, hikers, and anyone doing sustained physical activity in hot or humid weather.
guides.preferredAction: Adjust workload, rest breaks, clothing, and hydration while monitoring yourself and teammates for early symptoms.
guides.backupAction: If the schedule cannot change, shorten work, increase rest breaks, use every available shade or cooling option, and reduce intensity.
steps: Check heat alerts and move the hardest tasks to early morning or evening if possible.
Build up activity gradually over 1 to 2 weeks on the first hot days to support acclimatization.
Drink small frequent amounts of water throughout the activity rather than waiting for thirst.
Take regular breaks in shade or cooling areas.
Wear light breathable clothing, a hat, and sunscreen.
Stop early if cramps, dizziness, nausea, headache, or unusual fatigue appear.
warnings: Some medications, alcohol, and high humidity increase heat risk.
Heat illness can happen even when the temperature does not seem extreme if humidity is high and exertion is heavy.
whatNotToDo: Do not perform maximal-effort workouts or the heaviest labor during the hottest parts of the day if it can be avoided.
Do not ignore cramps, dizziness, or nausea.
redFlags: Confusion, chest pain, fainting, persistent vomiting, or symptoms that do not improve with rest and cooling.
preparednessTips: Use work-rest and hydration reminders tied to local heat alerts.
Encourage buddy systems in hot environments.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Risk factors and early symptoms for exertional heat illness.

sourceReferences.title: Protect Yourself From the Dangers of Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: General outdoor planning and heat behavior advice.

guides.title: Staying Warm and Dry in Cold Weather (Prevention)
guides.existingGuideReference: Staying Warm in a Cold Home Without Power
guides.quickAnswer: Dress in layers, stay dry, cover extremities, limit wind and wet exposure, and avoid overexertion that makes you sweat heavily.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever you must be outside in cold, windy, or freezing conditions and are not yet showing signs of hypothermia or frostbite.
guides.preferredAction: Use layered clothing, waterproof outer layers, hats, gloves, and insulated footwear, and seek warm shelter regularly.
guides.backupAction: If gear is limited, improvise insulation with dry spare clothing, blankets, plastic sheeting as a windbreak, and shared shelter.
steps: Dress in loose layers: moisture-wicking base, insulating middle, and windproof or waterproof outer layer.
Cover head, ears, hands, and feet with hat, gloves or mittens, and appropriate footwear.
Stay as dry as possible and change out of wet clothes quickly.
Limit time in strong wind and take frequent warm-up breaks indoors or in sheltered areas.
Eat regular meals and drink warm non-alcoholic fluids when available.
warnings: Being wet and exposed to wind speeds heat loss dramatically and can cause hypothermia even above freezing.
Tight clothing can reduce circulation and worsen cold injury risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not use alcohol to warm up.
Do not keep wearing wet socks, gloves, or base layers if dry replacements exist.
redFlags: Very cold, confused, or drowsy behavior.
Numb, pale, hard, or waxy skin on fingers, toes, ears, or nose.
preparednessTips: Pack extra dry socks, gloves, and a hat whenever you go out in cold weather.
Keep a cold-weather vehicle kit with blankets, spare clothing, snacks, and chemical hand warmers.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes CDC prevention advice on staying warm and dry.

sourceReferences.title: Winter Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical winter safety measures.

guides.title: Field Response to Mild Hypothermia
guides.quickAnswer: Get the person out of cold and wet conditions, remove wet clothes, warm the body core first, and seek medical care if symptoms are moderate or severe.
guides.whenToUse: When someone is shivering, cold, tired, or mildly confused after cold exposure but is still conscious and responsive.
guides.preferredAction: Move to warm shelter, replace wet clothes with dry ones, insulate the person, and warm chest, neck, head, and groin first.
guides.backupAction: If full shelter is not available, use any windbreak, dry layers, blankets, and body-to-body contact under loose dry coverings.
steps: Move the person to a warm room, vehicle with heat, or sheltered area out of wind and precipitation.
Gently remove wet clothing and replace it with dry clothing, blankets, or a sleeping bag.
Warm the center of the body first using blankets, warm packs, or body-to-body contact under dry layers.
Offer warm sweet non-alcoholic drinks if the person is awake and can swallow safely.
Keep the person comfortable and handle them gently.
Seek urgent medical care if symptoms persist, worsen, or mental status changes.
warnings: Moderate to severe hypothermia can be fatal without medical care.
Rough handling can be dangerous in more severe hypothermia.
whatNotToDo: Do not use very hot water or direct high heat.
Do not massage arms and legs vigorously.
Do not give alcohol.
redFlags: Stops shivering, becomes very confused, cannot walk, is very drowsy, has slowed breathing, or becomes unconscious.
preparednessTips: Include a hypothermia emergency flow in the app that emphasizes gentle handling and core rewarming.
Encourage users in cold regions to know local emergency numbers and warming center locations.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes CDC rewarming steps and warnings.

guides.title: Field Response to Frostbite
guides.quickAnswer: Protect the area from further cold, avoid walking on frostbitten feet, and rewarm in warm water only if you can prevent refreezing.
guides.whenToUse: When skin exposed to cold becomes numb, hard, pale, or waxy, especially on fingers, toes, nose, ears, or cheeks.
guides.preferredAction: Get to a warm place, remove wet or tight items, gently rewarm the area, and obtain medical care promptly.
guides.backupAction: If the tissue might refreeze, protect from further cold, pad and immobilize it, and delay active rewarming until refreezing risk is lower.
steps: Move the person to warm shelter as soon as possible.
Remove wet gloves, socks, or shoes, avoiding force on stuck items.
Do not walk on frostbitten feet or toes unless absolutely necessary.
Rewarm the area in warm water that feels comfortable to unaffected skin if refreezing risk is low.
Protect the area with loose clean covering and seek medical care as soon as practical.
warnings: Refreezing after thawing can cause severe tissue damage.
Rewarming can be painful but direct high heat can burn numb skin.
whatNotToDo: Do not rub frostbitten skin with snow or massage it.
Do not use direct dry heat from fires, stoves, or heating pads.
redFlags: Blistering, black or very dark skin, loss of sensation after rewarming, or large affected areas.
Frostbite plus hypothermia symptoms.
preparednessTips: Teach users that early tingling or numbness is a warning to stop and rewarm before frostbite develops.
Carry extra socks, gloves, and a dry insulating layer on all cold outings.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes basic field management of frostbite.

guides.title: Cold-Wet Exposure: Rain, Wind, and Chill
guides.existingGuideReference: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet
guides.quickAnswer: In cold, wet, windy conditions, staying dry and sheltered is as important as temperature; seek windbreaks, change wet clothes, and insulate from the ground.
guides.whenToUse: For hikers, outdoor workers, or stranded travelers in cool to cold weather with rain, wet snow, or strong wind.
guides.preferredAction: Reduce exposure by finding or creating shelter, changing into dry layers, and staying off cold ground or metal.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot fully shelter, improvise with a vehicle, tarp, overhang, or dense trees where safe, and maximize insulation with dry materials.
steps: Seek or create shelter from wind and precipitation.
Remove and replace wet clothing with dry items, or wring out clothes and add insulation if nothing else is available.
Sit or lie on insulating material instead of bare ground or metal.
Keep extremities covered and avoid sweating heavily into your clothes.
Monitor closely for early hypothermia and warm up early.
warnings: Hypothermia risk is high in cold-wet-windy conditions even when air temperatures are above freezing.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore wet clothing.
Do not rely only on exertion to stay warm; exhaustion increases risk.
redFlags: Confusion, extreme fatigue, stumbling, or other possible hypothermia signs.
preparednessTips: Promote waterproof outer layers and emergency rain gear in gear checklists.
Encourage bail-out routes or shelter options when traveling in cold-wet environments.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes wet clothing and warming.

sourceReferences.title: Winter Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical clothing and storm advice.

guides.title: Using AQI and Basic Smoke/Air Quality Precautions
guides.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash
guides.quickAnswer: Check AQI and reduce outdoor exertion, close windows, and protect sensitive groups first as air quality worsens.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever the air looks hazy, smells smoky or dirty, or authorities issue air-quality or wildfire-smoke alerts.
guides.preferredAction: Use AQI or local equivalent to guide behavior and reduce time and effort outdoors as conditions worsen.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot check AQI, treat visible smoke or strong odors as unhealthy and stay indoors with windows closed.
steps: Check AQI through official sources like AirNow or local agencies when possible.
If AQI is unhealthy for sensitive groups or worse, reduce prolonged or heavy outdoor exertion, especially for children, older adults, and people with heart or lung disease.
If AQI is unhealthy or higher, stay indoors as much as possible and use a portable air cleaner if available.
Avoid adding indoor pollution such as smoking, candles, frying, or vacuuming without HEPA filtration.
Follow local guidance on respirators such as N95-type masks if you must be outdoors in heavy smoke.
warnings: Poor air quality can worsen asthma, COPD, heart disease, and can trigger serious cardiorespiratory events.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on simple paper or cloth masks for fine smoke particles.
Do not exercise heavily outdoors during high smoke or pollution events.
redFlags: Difficulty breathing, chest pain, severe coughing, wheezing not relieved by usual medications, or confusion after smoke exposure.
preparednessTips: Users with asthma or heart disease should keep rescue medications accessible and follow clinician action plans.
Include AQI color explanations and offline behavior guidance in the app.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Smoke: A Guide for Public Health Officials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed AQI-linked public advice.

sourceReferences.title: Environmental and Health Impacts of Air Pollution: A Review
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes health effects of particulate air pollution.

guides.title: Protecting Yourself from Wildfire Smoke Indoors
guides.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash
guides.quickAnswer: Stay inside with windows and doors closed, create one cleaner-air room if possible, and avoid adding indoor pollution.
guides.whenToUse: During wildfire smoke events when outdoor air is visibly smoky or AQI is unhealthy or worse and evacuation is not currently ordered.
guides.preferredAction: Create a cleaner-air room at home, school, or work and limit outdoor time and exertion.
guides.backupAction: If you have no filter or cleaner, close windows and doors, seal obvious gaps, and avoid indoor particle-generating activities.
steps: Choose a room that can be closed off and is large enough for people to stay in.
Close windows and doors and seal obvious drafts if safe and temporary.
Use a portable HEPA air cleaner or approved box-fan filter setup if available and safe.
Set HVAC systems to recirculate indoor air if possible.
Avoid indoor activities that add particles such as smoking, frying, or vacuuming without HEPA filtration.
Leave for cleaner air elsewhere if the home remains very smoky or high-risk people become symptomatic.
warnings: Indoor air can quickly become unhealthy if windows remain open during smoke events or indoor particle sources are added.
whatNotToDo: Do not run systems that pull unfiltered outdoor air in during heavy smoke unless you have specific local guidance.
Do not use unvented gas, propane, or charcoal heaters indoors.
redFlags: Severe breathing difficulty, chest pain, or signs of heart attack or stroke during smoke events.
preparednessTips: Identify a cleaner-air room and obtain filters before wildfire season.
Build a checklist for assembling a basic cleaner-air space.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Smoke: A Guide for Public Health Officials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Indoor protection strategies and cleaner-air rooms.

guides.title: Dust, Ash, and Airborne Irritants After Storms, Eruptions, or Collapses
guides.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash
guides.quickAnswer: Avoid breathing dust and ash, use appropriate masks if available, stay upwind, and clean up gently with moisture to avoid re-suspending particles.
guides.whenToUse: After dust storms, volcanic ashfall, building collapses, or wildfires when particles cover surfaces or air quality is poor.
guides.preferredAction: Limit outdoor time, especially for sensitive people, use fitted respiratory protection when cleaning, and keep children away from heavy dust.
guides.backupAction: If no respirator is available, cover nose and mouth with a damp cloth or scarf and avoid vigorous cleanup that kicks up dust.
steps: Stay indoors as much as possible while dust or ash levels are high and close windows and doors.
If you must go outside or clean up, use a well-fitting respirator rated for fine particles if available.
Keep children, older adults, and people with lung or heart disease away from heavy dust and ash.
Lightly mist dusty surfaces and clean gently into sealed bags rather than dry sweeping.
Wash exposed skin and change clothes after cleanup.
warnings: Some dusts and ashes can contain toxic substances and fine particles that harm lungs and other organs.
whatNotToDo: Do not use leaf blowers or aggressive dry sweeping.
Do not allow children to play in ash or dust piles.
redFlags: Sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, severe coughing, or eye injury after dust or ash exposure.
preparednessTips: Store appropriate masks and eye protection if you live in dust-prone or volcanic regions.
Add local hazard-specific modules where relevant.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Smoke: A Guide for Public Health Officials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Cleanup and exposure reduction concepts for airborne particles.

sourceReferences.title: Environmental and Health Impacts of Air Pollution: A Review
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health effects of particulate matter and dust.

guides.title: Immediate Actions if Thunderstorm or Lightning Approaches Outdoors
guides.quickAnswer: Get inside a substantial building or hard-topped metal vehicle immediately; if you cannot, move away from high points, isolated trees, and water.
guides.whenToUse: At the first sound of thunder or sight of lightning when you are outdoors, especially in open areas or on high ground.
guides.preferredAction: Stop outdoor activity and go to a fully enclosed building or a hard-topped vehicle with the windows closed.
guides.backupAction: If no shelter or vehicle is available, move quickly to lower terrain away from tall objects and water, spread out if in a group, and minimize ground contact.
steps: At first thunder, stop swimming, boating, hiking on ridges, or field activity.
Head for the nearest substantial building with wiring and plumbing, or a hard-topped vehicle.
If caught on a ridge or open high ground, move downhill to lower terrain away from solitary trees and tall objects.
Get away from water, shorelines, metal fences, and railings.
warnings: Lightning can strike miles from the center of a storm; if you hear thunder, you are close enough to be struck.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter under isolated trees, in small open structures, or near tall metal objects.
Do not stay in or near bodies of water.
redFlags: Any lightning strike victim: call emergency services immediately and begin CPR if trained and needed.
preparednessTips: Follow the principle: when thunder roars, go indoors.
Plan escape routes from fields, beaches, lakes, and peaks.

sourceReferences.title: 7 lightning safety tips if you are caught outside during a thunderstorm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes NOAA-style recommendations on seeking shelter quickly.

guides.title: Everyday Sun Protection and UV Safety
guides.parentTopic: Sun Exposure and UV-Related Heat Stress
guides.quickAnswer: Limit midday sun, cover skin, use high-SPF sunscreen, and protect your eyes to reduce UV damage and heat stress.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever you will be outdoors for more than brief periods, especially around midday, at high altitude, or near reflective surfaces.
guides.preferredAction: Use shade, clothing, hats, sunglasses, and sunscreen together and move activities to lower-UV times where possible.
guides.backupAction: If sunscreen or full clothing is not available, prioritize shade, hats, and limiting time in direct sun.
steps: Plan outdoor activities for early morning or late afternoon when UV and heat are lower.
Wear long-sleeved tightly woven clothing, a broad-brimmed hat, and UV-blocking sunglasses.
Apply broad-spectrum high-SPF sunscreen to all exposed skin and reapply as directed.
Use shade whenever possible, especially during peak sun hours.
Drink water regularly to compensate for heat and sun exposure.
warnings: Excessive solar UV causes skin cancer, cataracts, and other eye damage and can contribute to heat stress.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on sunscreen alone without clothing and shade.
Do not use tanning as a way to protect your skin.
redFlags: Severe sunburn with large blisters, fever, chills, confusion, or signs of infection.
preparednessTips: Carry sunscreen, a hat, and sunglasses in outdoor kits.
Remind users that cloudy days can still have high UV.

sourceReferences.title: Health consequences of excessive solar UV radiation
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Risks and prevention recommendations for excessive UV exposure.

sourceReferences.title: Protect Yourself From the Dangers of Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Includes sunscreen, clothing, and shade advice.

guides.title: Basic Altitude Illness Prevention for Trekkers
guides.quickAnswer: Ascend gradually, include rest days, stay hydrated, avoid alcohol and overexertion, and stop or descend if symptoms appear.
guides.whenToUse: When planning or beginning treks, climbs, or travel above roughly 2,500 to 3,000 meters, especially if you are not acclimatized.
guides.preferredAction: Limit sleeping-altitude gain, schedule acclimatization days, and adjust plans based on symptoms.
guides.backupAction: If you must ascend faster, reduce exertion, monitor symptoms closely, and be ready to stop or descend early.
steps: Above roughly 3,000 meters, plan no more than about 300 to 500 meters increase in sleeping altitude per day and include rest days every few days.
Maintain steady hydration and eat regular nutritious meals.
Avoid alcohol and sedatives, especially at night.
Watch closely for headache, nausea, unusual fatigue, sleep disruption, or worsening symptoms and stop ascending if they intensify.
warnings: Rapid ascent increases the risk of altitude illness, which can become life-threatening if ignored.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore persistent or worsening symptoms while ascending.
Do not continue to higher sleeping altitude if you already have clear signs of altitude sickness.
redFlags: Severe headache, repeated vomiting, trouble walking straight, shortness of breath at rest, or altered behavior at altitude.
preparednessTips: Use region-specific altitude profiles and conservative ascent plans for routes such as the Himalaya.
Encourage pre-trip consultation with experienced providers about medications and higher-risk conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Health and Safety Tips for High Altitude Sickness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical trekking-oriented prevention advice.

guides.title: Weather-Related Red Flag Symptoms (Heat, Cold, Air, Lightning)
guides.parentTopic: When Weather Exposure Becomes a Medical Emergency
guides.quickAnswer: Confusion, chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, seizures, or loss of consciousness during weather exposure are medical emergencies.
guides.whenToUse: Any time someone becomes very unwell during extreme heat or cold, smoke or dust exposure, storms, or after a lightning strike.
guides.preferredAction: Recognize these signs quickly, move to the relevant emergency action guide, and call for help immediately.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot call right away, begin first aid and get help from others nearby as fast as possible.
steps: Check for red-flag symptoms such as confusion, inability to walk or talk normally, chest pain, severe breathing difficulty, seizures, or unresponsiveness.
In heat, suspect heat stroke if the person is very hot, confused, or collapsed and call emergency services while cooling.
In cold, suspect severe hypothermia if the person is very cold, confused, or not shivering and call for help while warming gently.
In smoke or dust, treat severe shortness of breath, chest tightness, or blue lips as emergencies.
After lightning, treat all strikes as emergencies even if the person initially seems okay.
warnings: Trying to tough it out with these symptoms can lead to death or permanent disability.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay calling for help to see whether severe symptoms go away.
Do not rely only on over-the-counter medications when severe symptoms are present.
redFlags: Confusion, collapse, chest pain, severe trouble breathing, seizures, or unresponsiveness after weather exposure.
preparednessTips: Build a unified Red Flags screen in the app that links to emergency action cards and local emergency contacts.
Allow a quick tap from any weather guide into this red-flag reference.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emergency heat-illness features and actions.
